next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Tommy was alone on the deck for the first time since Saturday morning. Monday afternoon, sun low enough to turn the lake copper, and maybe fifteen minutes before somebody came out to check on him or ask if he wanted another round of Mallick's questions or start talking about Martin in that careful tone everybody had found since the body came out of the bedroom.

He had dragged an Adirondack chair to the rail so he could put his feet up and look at the water. A warm can of Busch Light sat in both hands. He wasn't drinking it. He was palming it the way he palmed a football before a snap drill, not because the beer mattered but because empty hands felt worse.

Two nights since Martin. Not even three full days, and the house already felt like a locker room after a loss nobody knew how to explain. Men kept picking up cups, setting them down, starting sentences, dropping them halfway through. Paul had spent the morning in Martin's study doing whatever Paul did when he was processing, which usually meant turning grief into categories. Joel was somewhere in the house, still angry at a man who couldn't catch any more of it. Doug had been on the couch with the same magazine page since breakfast. Nate was in the basement with his phone, which Tommy was trying not to think about.

Mallick had gone back to Detroit Lakes. Working the case, supposedly. Tommy didn't buy it. That woman filed people the way Tommy filed opponent tendencies. Tells, mistakes, weak side, strong side. She'd be back.

The screen door opened and banged shut behind him.

"Hey." Cliff's voice. Smooth as...
open_hooks_to_carry: Paul in Martin's room going through his things — the intimacy and invasiveness of handling a dead friend's belongings
Paul finds Martin's will in the study: Martin left Tommy the lake house, not Paul
Memories of the Martin-Tommy dynamic across twenty-five years: protectiveness, tolerance, the way Martin always ensured Tommy came back
Paul begins to suspect a deeper bond — something that created obligation, not just friendship
The comedy in Paul's indignation should be real: he's also hurt that his best friend left the house to someone else
prev_tail_excerpt:
...eeded him to do. He'd owned the body and the wiping, and he'd gone honestly blank on the phone, the medications, and the safe. That was useful.

What he hadn't given her was a sequence that made the bedroom make sense. The room at the center of the whole mess still had too many hands in it and not enough witness.

She could sleep on likely. She could not close on likely.

The house settled around her, creaking the way old lake houses creak when the temperature drops and the wood contracts. Through the kitchen window she could see the stone path that led through the pines and birches to the dock, eighty yards of pale stone in the dark, ending at the wooden dock that ran thirty feet into the lake. The dock was a dark line against the darker water.

Maybe in the morning the bedroom would make ordinary sense. Maybe Nate got in and out between Paul's trips to the hallway. Maybe Tommy did. Maybe grief had narrowed Paul's world to Martin's face and left the rest of the room unrecorded. All of that was possible.

Possible was not the same as accounted for.

She closed the notebook, rinsed her coffee mug, dried it, and set it on the counter next to the coffeemaker because she'd want it first thing and it was already clean and why open the cabinet twice. She pressed the foil down over the casserole dish with both hands, just to have a task for them, then turned off the kitchen light and went upstairs to the guest bedroom the deputies had cleared for her.

The third step from the top creaked under her weight, and she wondered how many of the five had remembered to skip it in the dark.
secondary_character_beats: Paul's memories of Martin should include at least one that is purely funny and purely kind — the reader needs to grieve Martin too

setups_to_plant.description: Martin left Tommy the lake house in his will. This fact, combined with the fire document, will be the emotional trigger in Paul's confrontation with Tommy in Ch 17.

state_deltas_required: Paul's discovery that Martin's central loyalty was not to him but to Tommy
Paul's grief shifts from loss to suspicion — Martin was hiding something about Tommy, and the will is proof it wasn't casual
